coredata.dc:title: Dual-excitation in-lab-made device based on a handy UV lamp and GQDs-modified PADs for simultaneous determination of acetaminophen and its endocrine disrupting impurity 4-nitrophenol
coredata.dc:creator: El-Shaheny, Rania
Al-Khateeb, Lateefa A.
El-Maghrabey, Mahmoud
coredata.dc:description: In this study, we first propose novel and intelligent strategies for simultaneous estimation of acetaminophen (AC) and its potential toxic impurity 4-nitrophenol (NP) using an in-lab-made device as well as a benchtop fluorometry technique. The developed approaches depend on the dual-excitation of fluorescent graphene quantum dots (GQDs) at 240 and 350 nm, and measurement of the characteristic emission at 440 nm. AC efficiently quenches the fluorescence of GQDs when excited at 240 nm, while NP quenches the fluorescence of GQDs when excited at 350 nm. The mechanism involved in the quenching has been established to be the inner filter effect via absorption of the incident excitation light by the quenchers. An in-lab-made device that integrates a dual-wavelength handy UV lamp and a GQDs modified paper-based analytical device (PAD) confined with a permanent ink marker has been developed. Imaging of the PAD was performed by a smartphone, and image processing permitted the selective estimation of AC and NP. This technique offers an affordable, easily fabricated, and widely accessible device for application in limited-resources laboratories. The benchtop approach permitted the determination of AC and NP over the range of 0.1–20 and 0.1–2.0 µg/mL, while the in-lab-made device approach allowed AC and NP determination within the ranges of 0.04–6.0 and 0.04–2.0 µg/detection zone, respectively. The two approaches have been successfully validated for the estimation of AC and NP in pharmaceutical preparations. The proposed strategies are the first to utilize a single nanomaterial for simultaneous analysis of two analytes adapting the dual excitation technique.

head.ce:title: Dual-excitation in-lab-made device based on a handy UV lamp and GQDs-modified PADs for simultaneous determination of acetaminophen and its endocrine disrupting impurity 4-nitrophenol

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, we first propose novel and intelligent strategies for simultaneous estimation of acetaminophen (AC) and its potential toxic impurity 4-nitrophenol (NP) using an in-lab-made device as well as a benchtop fluorometry technique. The developed approaches depend on the dual-excitation of fluorescent graphene quantum dots (GQDs) at 240 and 350 nm, and measurement of the characteristic emission at 440 nm. AC efficiently quenches the fluorescence of GQDs when excited at 240 nm, while NP quenches the fluorescence of GQDs when excited at 350 nm. The mechanism involved in the quenching has been established to be the inner filter effect via absorption of the incident excitation light by the quenchers. An in-lab-made device that integrates a dual-wavelength handy UV lamp and a GQDs modified paper-based analytical device (PAD) confined with a permanent ink marker has been developed. Imaging of the PAD was performed by a smartphone, and image processing permitted the selective estimation of AC and NP. This technique offers an affordable, easily fabricated, and widely accessible device for application in limited-resources laboratories. The benchtop approach permitted the determination of AC and NP over the range of 0.1–20 and 0.1–2.0 µg/mL, while the in-lab-made device approach allowed AC and NP determination within the ranges of 0.04–6.0 and 0.04–2.0 µg/detection zone, respectively. The two approaches have been successfully validated for the estimation of AC and NP in pharmaceutical preparations. The proposed strategies are the first to utilize a single nanomaterial for simultaneous analysis of two analytes adapting the dual excitation technique.

ce:section.ce:para: Great attention is currently paid to carbon-based nanostructures for diverse applications in multiple fields. Though, forms like carbon nanotubes, graphene, and fullerenes show poor solubility in water and weak fluorescence in the visible region. On the other hand, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are water-soluble and highly fluorescent nanomaterial that represents a focus of research in this field. GQDs possess a carbonaceous skeleton with abundant surface oxygen-bearing function groups that convey excellent aqueous solubility [1]. In addition to their strong photoluminescent properties [2], GQDs are characterized by biosafety, environmental safety, and photostability [1]. Thus, diverse applications of GQDs have been reported as fluorescence sensors for the determination of metal ions, drugs, organic compounds, and macromolecules [2–5]. Generally, the application of GQDs as a fluorescence sensor depends on quenching or inhibiting the quenching of GQDs' fluorescence. The mechanisms involved in quenching of GQDs fluorescence may include the inner filter effect (IFE), static quenching, dynamic quenching, photoinduced electron transfer, or fluorescence resonance energy transfer [3]. However, the simultaneous fluorescence analysis of multiple analytes by a single nanomaterial is very scarce, where the few published methods for this aim require the incorporation of two or more types of nanomaterials [6,7]. These additional steps introduce some drawbacks such as higher cost, tedious procedure, and longer time. Thus, there is a persistent need for a simpler and more convenient tactic to overcome these problems and facilitate the simultaneous fluorescence analysis using GQDs solely.

In this study, we proposed a new approach for simultaneous determination of acetaminophen (AC) and its synthesis impurity 4-nitrophenol (NP) ( Fig. 1a) using GQDs only by adapting a double-scanning fluorometry (DSF) technique. AC (N-acetyl-p-aminophenol) is considered the most frequently administered analgesic and antipyretic for adults and pediatric usage. It is used for treating moderate to mild pain and fever [8,9]. There are hundreds of medicines that contain AC as an active ingredient. It is approved by the FDA as an over-the-counter medication where approximately 50% of AC units are sold as over-the-counter [10]. NP is an AC-related impurity and synthesis precursor. It is specified in the British [11] and United States Pharmacopoeias [12] as an impurity of AC. The synthesis of AC typically comprises the reduction of NP as a starting material followed by a series of steps to produce AC. Thus, NP could exist as an impurity during the crystallization of AC [13]. NP has a high potential as a carcinogenic, teratogenic, and mutagenic compound [14,15]. Furthermore, it is recognized as an endocrine disruptor [16]. Meanwhile, Keshavarz et al. [13] addressed that other potential impurities seldom occur and are not hazardous substances. These facts urge the development of analytical methods for simultaneous determination of AC and NP and to detect traces of NP in AC pharmaceutical formulations. To this end, few analytical methods were developed for this purpose, such as micellar liquid chromatography [17] and HPLC using a mixed-mode reversed-phase/cation exchange stationary phase [18] or a dual-mode gradient elution [19]. However, all these methods lack the sensitivity needed for trace impurity detection. Additionally, chromatographic techniques generally need large volumes of organic solvents and involve long preparation and analysis times. On the other hand, fluorometry is a sensitive and selective technique that is known for its ability for trace analysis. However, the analytical literature lacks any fluorescence method for simultaneous determination of AC and NP since the two compounds are non-fluorescent. Yet, the analysis of AC has been solely reported via measuring the fluorescence of its oxidation product obtained by treatment with hypochlorite in carbonate buffer at 80 °C [20], and another fluorescence method has been reported for the determination of AC in solid state [21]. Some fluorometric methods have been applied for NP based on quenching of the fluorescence of some nanomaterials [22–24]. However, none of these methods succeeded in the simultaneous fluorometric analysis of NP and AC.

By exploring the photoluminescence properties of GQDs, we noticed that it has two excitation wavelengths at 240 and 350 nm and a single emission wavelength at 440 nm (Fig. 1b). Meanwhile, AC has a maximum UV-absorption at 240 nm without any absorbance over the range of 350–440 nm, while NP shows UV-absorbance at 350 nm and a non-significant absorbance at 240 or 440 nm (Fig. 1b). Inspired by these observations, we designed a new sensor for the simultaneous determination of AC and NP using GQDs. The new sensor is based on turning off the fluorescence of GQDs by AC when excited at 240 nm via IFE without interference from the co-existing NP. Meanwhile, NP quenches the fluorescence of GQDs by excitation at 350 via IFE with no interference from AC. Thus, selective determination of AC and NP can be achieved via DSF at 240/440 nm and 350/440 nm, respectively. Despite the absorption maximum of NP is not exactly at 350 nm, NP shows sufficient absorbance at 350 nm that allowed its sensitive quantitative determination. The idea of the proposed approach is illustrated in Fig. 1c.

Paper-based analytical devices (PADs) have emerged as a striking analytical tool for point-of-need measurement of various target compounds. Such a platform offers many distinct advantages that contribute to their wide popularity, such as cheapness, minimum consumption of sample and reagents, ease of application, safe disposability, lightweight, portability, and suitability for in-field analysis. Thus, PADs have found numerous applications in environmental monitoring [25], diagnostics [26], forensic analysis [9,27], and food analysis [28]. Furthermore, some methods integrating PADs, nanomaterials, and smartphones for image’s capturing and processing to generate analytical signals have been reported [29]. The fabrication of PADs relies on using cellulose-based filter papers as hydrophilic substrates, whereas hydrophobic barriers are applied to create detection areas mostly using wax ink printer, photolithography, screen printing, or micro embossing [30]. However, these techniques mandate the use of benchtop apparatus such as special printers, air compressors, or laser cutter in addition to the need for some extra steps such as heating or UV-treatment for the creation of the hydrophobic barriers [30].

Within this context, we were motivated to adapt the developed DSF technique to design a valid and easily fabricated paper-based platform integrated with an in-lab-made UV-imaging chamber for simultaneous determination of AC and NP. We described the fabrication of a PAD by an equipmentless method depends on the pen-on-paper technique via freehand drawing of the hydrophobic barriers with a permanent marker on a paper substrate. The entire PAD fabrication is accomplished through a single-step procedure, does not necessitate either a special instrument, high experience, or any expensive tools. The permanent marker used herein is customarily used to label children’s school supplies and similar stuff, and it can be easily purchased from any stationery shop worldwide at a very low expense (about 1.0 $). Moreover, to make the PADs more affordable, we used qualitative filter papers as a substrate instead of the commonly used and more expensive chromatography papers. The patterning method exhibited good reproducibility, excellent confinement, and technical practicability for fabricating flexible devices. The GQDs solution has been coated on the detection zones for sensing AC and NP. To quantitatively read and interpret the fluorescence signal from the PAD, a smartphone-based read-out device (SRD) has been assembled combined with a handy UV-lamp (254/365 nm) as excitation source. A simple illustration of the SRD is displayed in Fig. 2. The PAD images taken by the SRD are processed by a free smartphone software to get the analytical signal (Fig. 2). Imaging of the PAD under UV-illumination at 254 nm permitted the quantification of AC without any interference from NP. Similarly, the images taken for the PAD at 365 nm enabled the determination of NP without any interference from AC.

Thus, the aims of this study can be summarized as (i) establishing the first DSF technique for simultaneous determination of AC and NP using GQDs single material as a fluorescence sensor, (ii) adapting the DSF technique to establish the first lab-on-paper platform for simultaneous estimation of the two analytes using a cheap and easy pen-on-paper approach, (iii) designing and fabrication of an inexpensive and easily fabricated in-lab-made SRD that can be afforded even in poor-resources laboratories, and (iv) application of the developed techniques for analysis of pharmaceutical formulations.

Fluorescence measurements were done using an FP-6600 spectrofluorometer from Jasco Co. (Tokyo, Japan) installed with a 150 W Xenon lamp and a 1 cm quartz cell. The slit widths for excitation and emission monochromators were 5 and 10 nm, respectively. A V-6600 double-beam Jasco spectrophotometer was used for absorption measurement using a pair of matched quartz cells. FTIR-4200 Jasco spectrophotometer was used for scanning the FTIR spectrum of GQDs, and Zetasizer Nano ZS from Malvern Instruments Ltd. (Worcestershire, UK) was used for zeta potential measurement. Transmission electron microscope (TEM) from Jeol Ltd. (Tokyo, Japan) was used for imaging of GQDs and characterization of its morphology and particle size. Permanent markers from different companies (Sakura Color Products Corporation, Japan (M1), Teranishi Chemical Industry Co, Ltd., Japan (M2), and Zebra Co., Ltd, Japan (M3) were purchased from a local stationery shop. A combo circle template ruler, 976 02, from Staedtler Co., Japan, was used for drawing the design of the detection zones of the PADs.

Pure powder of AC was kindly donated by EIPICO Co., 10th Ramadan City, Egypt and pure NP (99%, Lot No. WTQ1131) was purchased from Wako Pure Chemical Industries Co. Ltd (Osaka, Japan). Calonal tablets (200 mg AC/tablets), Ayumi Pharmaceutical Co. Ltd., Japan, were purchased from a local pharmacy. A suspension of GQDs (1 mg/mL in water) was bought from Sigma Aldrich Co. (St. Louis, USA). DISMIC 25 CS disposable cellulose acetate membrane filters with a pore size of 0.2 µm (Lot No. 901301CD) were purchased from Advantec® Co. (Tokyo, Japan). Whatman Grade 2 filter papers were used.

Individual standard solutions of AC (200 µg/mL) and NP (100 µg/mL) were prepared in deionized water and stored in the refrigerator at 4 °C. Working solutions of GQDs (100 and 10 µg/mL) was freshly prepared in deionized water.

The in-lab-made SRD was assembled by fitting an AS ONE handy 254/365 nm UV Lamp SLUV-6 (Osaka, Japan) into a dark box that has a small upper hole to fit a smartphone adapter. A small tray covered with black origami paper was fixed on the floor of the imaging box to secure the position of the PAD in all measurements. Samsung Galaxy S9 smartphone with 12 megapixels camera was used for capturing and processing the images of the PAD. Image processing was performed using the RGB color detector program, Version 2, 2021, downloaded from the Google play store. Fig. 2 shows a schematic representation of the SRD and the main steps of image processing. ImageJ software (NIH, Maryland, USA) run on an HP laptop was also used for image processing to be compared with the smartphone processing using the RGB color detector program.

Different volumes of the standard solutions of AC and NP were accurately measured and transferred to two groups of 10 mL calibrated flasks so that yielding final concentrations within the ranges of 0.1–20 and 0.1–2.0 µg/mL, respectively. GQDs solution was added (500 µL, 10 µg/mL) to the 10 mL flasks giving final concentration of 0.5 µg/mL GQDs. The volumes were completed with deionized water, solutions were thoroughly mixed, and the fluorescence intensity (F) was measured at λex/λem of 240/440 and 350/440 nm for AC and NP analysis, respectively. Blank experiments were run simultaneously, omitting the drug, and the fluorescence intensity (F0) was also measured. Calibration graphs were constructed by plotting (ln F0/F) versus the drug concentration (µg/mL), and the regression equations were calculated.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Simultaneous determination of AC and NP
ce:section.ce:para: Different volumes of the standard solutions of AC and NP were measured and transferred into a series of 10 mL calibrated flasks to prepare mixtures containing different concentrations of the two compounds. The fluorescence intensity of each solution was double scanned at λex/λem of 240/440 and 350/440 nm. The concentration of AC was calculated by substituting the F values obtained at λex/λem of 240/440 nm in the corresponding regression equation while the concentrations of NP were calculated by substituting the F values obtained at λex/λem of 350/440 nm in the corresponding regression equation. Percentage recoveries and standard deviation were calculated for each compound.

Correction of the potential IFE was carried out to evaluate its role in quenching of GQDs fluorescence. Two separate series of samples (AC and NP) were prepared as per the procedure for calibration graph and the fluorescence intensity of each sample was measured (Fobs.) as described in “Section 2.3.1.”. The UV-absorbance of AC and NP solutions at the same concentrations was measured spectrophotometrically at the respective wavelengths of excitation and emission (Aex and Aem, respectively). The corrected fluorescence intensity (Fcorr) after subtracting the IFE is calculated according to Eq. (1) [31]:
(1) Fcorr=Fobsx 10(Aex+Aem)/2

Next, the %suppressed efficiency (%E) was calculated for both Fobs. and Fcorr using Eq. 2 [31], then plotted versus quencher concentration [Q].
(2) %E =[1-(F/F0)]x100

Stern-Volmer analysis was conducted to inspect the potential role of dynamic or static mechanism in the quenching of GQDs fluorescence by the two analytes. Three sets of AC samples and three sets of NP samples were prepared according to the procedure described for calibration graphs (Section 2.3.1.). The samples were incubated in a thermostatically controlled water bath at the specified temperature (293, 303, and 313 K) then analyzed. Plots of F0/F versus quencher concentration [Q] were derived according to Eq. (3) [32], and the quenching constant K was calculated from the slope of the obtained curves.
(3) F0/F =1+K [Q]

ce:section.ce:section-title: Procedures for determination of AC and NP by the PAD

ce:section.ce:para: The steps of fabrication of the PAD as well as the PAD black cover are detailed in Section “S.1.” (Supplementary Materials). Fig. S1 (Supplementary Materials) illustrates the design of the PAD and the black cover.

The images of the PADs captured with the smartphone were analyzed with the RGB color detector free software to obtain the individual coordinates of three color models: RGB, HSL, and HSV. The following steps were applied for image analysis: (i) the image of the PAD was loaded to the software, (ii) the color values for each detection zone were analyzed by tapping a circular detection area by the touch panel and direct reading of the values of the individual attributes of each color space from the results panel, and (iii) the value of G coordinate of the RGB color model was directly read as the analytical signal. Fig. 2 illustrates the main steps of image processing by the RGB color detector software.

The used PAD included a total of eight detection zones (Fig. S1, Supplementary Materials), therefore permitting for eight distinct measurements per one device. Fifteen µL of GQDs solution (100 µg/mL) was added to each detection zone and allowed to dry at room temperature. The AC assay was carried out by spotting 20 µL of the standard drug solutions individually on seven detection zones of the PAD with a micropipette. Twenty µL of deionized water was spotted on the eighth zone that served as the blank zone. Likewise, the assay of NP was done by spotting 20 µL of the standard solutions individually on seven detection zones while the eighth zone is the blank. The PADs were left to dry at room temperature (10–15 min), covered with the black origami paper cover, and stapled to fix the cover; then, the PADs were transferred to the SRD. For AC assay, an image of the PAD was taken by a Samsung Galaxy S9 smartphone under UV-illumination at 254 nm, while NP assay was accomplished by imaging the PAD under UV-illumination at 365 nm. Each experiment was independently repeated three times. Next, the images were subjected to image analysis to get the values of green coordinate G, then
(
G 0
G
− 1 )
were plotted versus drug concentration (µg/detection zone) to construct the calibration curve, where G0 is the G value for the blank. The regression equations were calculated.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Simultaneous determination of AC and NP by the PADs
ce:section.ce:para: A schematic illustration of the PAD used in this experiment is shown in Fig. S1 (Supplementary Materials). The reliability of the PADs for simultaneous assay of AC and NP was tested as follows: (i) 15 µL of GQDs solution (100 µg/mL) was added to each detection zone and allowed to dry at room temperature, (ii) 20 µL of AC standard solutions were spotted on the first three zones (CH-A), (iii) 20 µL of NP standard solutions were spotted on the second three zones (CH-B), and (iv) 20 µL of AC/NP mixed standard solutions were spotted on the third three zones (CH-C). The last zone is the blank zone, and it was spiked with 20 µL of deionized water instead of test analytes. The PAD was allowed to dry at room temperature (10–15 min), then transferred to the SRD and imaged twice by the smartphone under UV-illumination at 254 nm and 365 nm. The images were analyzed and processed as described earlier, and the values of G were used to calculate the concentrations of the cited drugs from the regression equations.

Ten tablets were weighed, milled into a fine powder, and homogenized. An amount of the powder equivalent to 200 mg AC was weighed, transferred into 50 mL calibrated flasks, and diluted to final volume with deionized water. The mixture was sonicated for 30 min then filtered with a 0.2 µm disposable cellulose acetate membrane filter. An aliquot of 0.25 mL of the filtrate was transferred into a 10 mL volumetric flask and diluted with water to prepare a standard solution (100 µg/mL). Different volumes of this solution (100–600 µL) were taken into a set of 10 mL calibrated flasks to prepare a set of solutions of different concentrations. The samples were analyzed according to the procedure described in Section “2.3.2″ for the determination of AC and detection of potentially existing NP by the DSF method. Furthermore, a set of samples were analyzed by the PAD protocol by spotting 20 µL of each sample on the detection zones pre-coated with 15 µL GQDs solution, then the PAD was allowed to dry at room temperature. The PAD was transferred to the SRD and imaged twice by the smartphone under UV-illumination at 254 nm and 365 nm. The images were analyzed and processed as described earlier. The recoveries, %, and the RSD, %, were calculated.

The FTIR spectrum of the GQDs ( Fig. 3a) shows a broad peak at 3392 and a strong peak at 1066 cm−1 that are assigned to stretching vibrations of an alcoholic –OH and C–OH, respectively. The two peaks at 2900 and 2985 cm−1 are the stretching of the C–H bond of alkane, while the peak at 1637 cm−1 is assigned to the alkenyl CC stretch. To sum up, this result indicates that the GQDs are hydroxylated. Zeta potential of the GQDs was found equals to − 20.1 mv (Fig. S2, Supplementary Materials), confirming this result where the surface -OH groups are deprotonated imparting negative charges. In sequence, the surface negative charges contribute to the solubility of GQDs in water and their stability [33]. Further investigation of the morphology and particle size of GQDs has been conducted via TEM imaging. As noticed from the TEM image (Fig. 3b), GQDs are spherical having a uniform particle size with an average diameter of 2.25 ± 0.93 nm.

The absorption spectrum of GQDs showed two peaks at 240 nm and 350 nm (Fig. 1b). The absorption peak at 240 nm is attributed to the π–π * transition of the CC of the aromatic core structure of GQDs, while the peak at 350 nm is assigned to the n-π * transition of the oxygen of the surface –OH groups [33]. The GQDs show a characteristic fluorescence emission band typically at 440 nm when excited at either 240 or 350 nm. This emission is more intense using excitation at 350 nm, whilst excitation at 240 nm returns a slightly less intense fluorescence (Fig. 1b).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Simultaneous determination of AC and NP by the DSF technique

ce:section.ce:para: Different variables that potentially influence the selectivity and quenching of GQDs fluorescence by the two targeted compounds were carefully explored. The influence of pH was investigated over a wide range (1.5–9.5) using 0.1 M HCl, acetate, and borate buffers. The main factor considered in this study is achieving the best selectivity. It is noticed from Fig. 4a that, AC efficiently quenched the fluorescence of GQDs when excited at 240 nm; meanwhile, negligible or no quenching was observed using λex of 350 nm over the pH range of 1.5–7.0 since AC exhibits no absorbance at 350–440 nm within this pH range. Yet, at higher pH, a significant interference takes place when λex350 nm is used. This is attributed to the bathochromic shift of the absorption spectrum of AC causing a significant absorbance at 350 nm [34] that leads to IFE and interference of AC with NP determination in alkaline pH.

On the other hand, the extent of quenching of GQDs fluorescence by NP using λex350 nm increased steadily with increasing the pH. Nevertheless, the minimum interference from NP using λex 240 nm was established at pH 7.0 (Fig. 4b). This behavior is attributed to the bathochromic shift of the absorption spectrum of NP in the alkaline medium [35,36]; thus, it exhibits a significant absorption at 440 nm causing fluorescence quenching by absorbing the emitted radiation. Thus, pH 7.0 was selected as the optimum for the best selectivity where no significant interference from each drug with the other was detected.

Additionally, the quenching experiments were explored in some solvents, including water, methanol, and ethanol, all at pH 7.0. The optimum solvent in terms of quenching efficiency, cost-efficiency, safety, and availability is water. Furthermore, the influence of the concentration of GQDs was investigated over the range of 0.2–0.6 µg/mL (final concentrations). The effect of GQDs concentration on the quenching efficiency was not drastic as demonstrated in Table 1. A final concentration of 0.5 µg/mL was selected as the optimum in further studies.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Simultaneous analysis of AC and NP via DSF
ce:section.ce:para: As demonstrated in Fig. 5a, the fluorescence intensity of GQDs is efficiently quenched by AC when excited at 240 nm, whereas the presence of different concentrations of NP does not influence the quenching efficiency. On the other hand, NP quenches the fluorescence of GQDs when excited at 350 without any interference from AC, as illustrated in Fig. 5b. This proves the principle of the proposed sensor for simultaneous analysis of AC and NP using the DSF technique. Concentration-dependent quenching of GQDs fluorescence upon addition of increasing concentrations of AC (λex/λem 240/440 nm) and NP (λex/λem 350/440 nm) is evident in Fig. 6a & 6b, respectively. Calibration curves were constructed for AC and NP using their corresponding selective excitation wavelengths, 240 and 350 nm, respectively. Excellent linear relationships (r ≥ 0.9983) were established by plotting Ln F0/F versus concentration (µg/mL) over the concentration ranges of 0.1–20.0 µg/mL of AC and 0.1–2.0 µg/mL of NP (Fig. 6c & 6d, respectively) and the regression equations are summarized in Table 2. The calculation of the limits of detection (LOD) and limits of quantification (LOQ) was done for the two compounds as 3.3 σ/a and 10 σ/a, respectively, where “σ” is the standard deviation of the response and “a” is the slope of the calibration curve [37,38]. As sown in Table 2, the calculated LODs were found to be 30 and 32 ng/mL for AC and NP, respectively, demonstrating the high sensitivity of the proposed method. The reliability and applicability of the new technique were tested by analysis of different mixtures containing AC and NP. The excellent %recoveries of both compounds and small RSD, %, ( Table 3) confirm the selectivity of the new technique and its ability to determine each compound in the presence of the other without any interference.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Elucidation of the mechanism of GQDs fluorescence quenching by AC and NP
ce:section.ce:para: The mechanism of quenching of GQDs fluorescence by the two analytes was thoroughly investigated. It is obvious from Fig. 1b that there is a sufficient overlap region between the UV-absorption spectrum of AC and the excitation spectrum of GQDs at 240 nm, while no overlap region is observed with the second excitation peak (350 nm) or with the emission peak. Similarly, the NP absorption spectrum possesses an overlap region with the excitation spectrum of GQDs peaking at 350 nm without overlapping with the excitation peak at 240 or with the emission peak. Thus, it is believed that the primary inner filter effect (IFE) already exists where each quencher absorbs the cited excitation light. Primary IFE occurs when the absorption spectrum of the quencher overlapped with the excitation spectrum of fluorophore (GQDs). Thus, primary IFE takes place via attenuation of the excitation beam by the quencher in solution causing a reduction of the emitted fluorescence intensity [3,39].

To verify this theory, IFE was further corrected by applying the procedure described in Section 2.3.3. The suppressed efficiency (%E) for Fobs and Fcorr for AC and NP are illustrated in Fig. 7a & 7b, respectively. It is implied that the fluorescence quenching is exclusively attributed to the IFE since the %E is almost lost for Fcorr (i.e., after removing IFE) [31].

To further verify the quenching mechanism, Stern-Volmer analysis was done at increasing temperatures (293, 303, 313 K) [32]. The quenching constants (K) and correlation coefficients are summarized in Table S1 (Supplementary Materials). It is noted that the K values exhibited very slight variation. It is clear from the results shown in Fig. 7c & d that the Stern-Volmer plots are linear, but their slopes are not temperature-dependent. This suggests that the quenching mechanism is neither static nor dynamic [32]. Further evidence that elucidates that the static quenching has no role in the quenching process is the absorption spectra of GQDs and GQDs-AC, and GQDs-NP mixtures measured versus the corresponding drug as a blank solution. It is implicit from Fig. S3 (Supplementary Materials) that the three spectra are identical and exactly superimposed, which means that no new compound is formed as a result of the interaction of the analytes with GQDs. This result excludes any contribution from static quenching [32].
To sum up, these results evidenced that the mechanism of the quenching of GQDs fluorescence by AC and NP is IFE.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Simultaneous determination of AC and NP by the PAD and the in-lab-made device

ce:section.ce:para: The newly designed PAD has been inspected as an alternate approach to the DSF technique that was based on benchtop instruments. In this investigation, we adapted an innovative and simple pen-on-paper technique to develop a new affordable and easily-made PAD. The new design is based on using a permanent marker that is traditionally used to label children’s school supplies and similar kinds of stuff to plot the pattern of the detection zones on the filter paper substrate. The permanent marker is very cheap and commercially available in all countries in stationery shops at a very affordable price. The main advantages of this approach are simplicity, rapidness, equipmentless nature, cheapness, and the feasibility of application in developing countries and limited-resources laboratories.

Experimental studies have been conducted to optimize the designed PAD. The confining capability of permanent markers from different manufacturers has been investigated by plotting circular zones on the filter paper and dropping a test solution of 30 µL of pink-colored rhodamine 6G aqueous solution (100 µg/mL) to ease rapid visualization. Three markers (M1, M2, and M3) have been investigated, with two of them (M2 and M3) having twin tip with different line widths. The ascribed line widths are 1 mm for M1, 0.5 and 0.8–1.2 mm for M2, and 0.7 and 0.3 mm for M3. The permanent markers M1 and M3 exhibited excellent confining ability. On the other hand, M2 was unable to properly confine the solution showing a remarkable leakage by drawing with either the 0.5 or the 0.8–1.2 mm tip ( Fig. 8a). This may be attributed to the different compositions of the ink where this marker is commercialized as magic ink® with a special formula. Thus, the M1 permanent marker was selected for the design of our PAD.

Furthermore, the confining ability of the designed PAD was tested for single- and double-sided drawing with M1. However, it was observed that some leakage takes place when the single-sided drawing is adapted (Fig. 8b). Thus, a double-sided drawing is recommended to guarantee complete liquid confinement.

For the selection of the optimal substrate for the fabrication of the PAD, we compared two different filter papers, including Grade 2 Whatman and Grade 2 Advantec filter papers. As seen in Fig. 8c, the two filter papers have an excellent performance where a complete liquid restriction is obvious. This behavior confirmed the flexibility and accessibility of the developed PAD. In the next work, we used the Whatman filter paper for the fabrication of PADs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of image processing and analytical signal
ce:section.ce:para: Experiments were conducted to optimize smartphone image processing. The individual values of three color spaces: RGB (red, green, and blue), HSL (hue, saturation, and lightness), and HSV (hue, saturation, and value), were directly obtained for each detection zone in the PAD using the RGB color detector software. The suitability of each color value as the quantitative analytical signal was tested by plotting the calibration curve and evaluating the obtained linearity. The best color value to be used as the quantitative analytical signal was the green coordinate (G) since it gave the best linearity and the highest sensitivity.

Further experiments were performed to ensure the uniform UV-irradiation of all of the detection zones/PAD during imaging in the SRD. The PAD was tested by coating each detection zone with 15 µL of GQDs solution and successive imaging under UV-irradiation at 254 and 365 nm. The images were processed with the RGB color detector software to get the G0 values. The mean G0 values at 254 and 365 nm were 178.25 ± 0.56 and 146.0 ± 0.54, respectively (Fig. S4, Supplementary Material). The small values of SD confirm the uniformity of UV-irradiation of the detection zones establishing excellent reliability of the developed technique and the SRD.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Simultaneous analysis of AC and NP by the developed PAD
ce:section.ce:para:
A study was conducted to evaluate the assay performance for the determination of AC and NP via the PAD. Quantitation of AC depends on the analysis of the images captured under UV-illumination at 254 nm, while NP quantification depends on the analysis of the images taken at 365 nm ( Fig. 9a). Analytical calibration curves were constructed by plotting
(
G 0
G
− 1 )
versus the corresponding analyte concentration (µg/zone). As shown in Fig. 9c and d, excellent linearity (r ≥ 0.9973) was obtained over the concentration ranges of 0.04–6.0 and 0.04–2.0 µg/zone of AC and NP, where the linear relationships are described by regression equations cited in Table 2. The LOD and LOQ were calculated as 3.3σ/a and 10 σ/a, respectively. As shown in Table 2, the calculated LODs were found to be 13 and 9 ng/zone for AC and NP, respectively, indicating the high sensitivity of the PAD method. The excellent performance of the SRD for selective recognition of AC and NP is evident from the obtained results and the images shown in Fig. 9a.

The functionality of the proposed PAD has also been examined by applying the device for the simultaneous determination of AC and NP mixtures. It is apparent from the images of the PAD (Fig. 9b) that, no interference from each analyte with the analysis of the other. The determination results are abridged in Table 3. The average recoveries, %, of AC and NP are 97.49 ± 2.44% and 95.89 ± 2.76%, respectively. Thus, it can be concluded that the PAD coupled with 254/365 nm double-imaging and smartphone image processing is very well-suited as an efficient technique for the simultaneous determination of AC and NP.

Moreover, the reliability of the RGB color detector software has been examined by comparing its results with the results achieved using the widely used ImageJ software run with a personal computer by linear plotting of the obtained concentrations calculated by the two software. The obtained linear graph presented in Fig. S5 (Supplementary Materials) showed high correlation coefficients (≥0.9995), which confirmed the excellent matching between the performances of the RGB color detector software and ImageJ software.

A study of different validation parameters was conducted to confirm the reliability of the proposed approach according to the ICH guidelines [37,38]. The accuracy and the precision of the developed DSF and PAD methods were investigated by triplicate determination of three different concentrations of AC and NP within the same day (intra-day) and on three consecutive days (inter-day) using the DSF and PAD methods (Table S2, Supplementary Materials). The accuracy was calculated as found, %, and it showed good results ranged from 97.55%–101.43% for the DSF method and 94.18–102.13% for the PAD method. Regarding methods’ precision, it was expressed as the relative standard deviation (RSD, %) on an intra- and inter-day basis, and it was ≤ 1.55 and ≤ 2.63 for the DSF and PAD methods, respectively, as shown in Table S2. These results demonstrate the accuracy, repeatability, and reliability of the developed DSF and PAD methods for the determination of AC and NP under different operation circumstances [37,38].

The possible interference that may take place due to probably co-existing substances was also investigated. It is evinced from the data summarized in Table 4 that no significant interference is encountered from different substances such as Mg2+, Na+, Ca2+, K+, SO4 2-, Cl-, glucose, or glycine with the determination of both AC and NP. The change, %, of the response of GQDs at λex 240 and 350 nm did not exceed 1% and − 2.9%, respectively. Besides, the possible interference from the commonly co-administered or co-formulated drug, pseudoephedrine, was investigated, and the experiments confirmed that no significant interference exists even at high concentrations up to 20 µg/mL (0.12 mM) of pseudoephedrine (Table 4) since the change, % of the response of GQDs at λex 240 and 350 nm was ≤ −0.62 and − 1.17%, respectively. These results demonstrated the high specificity of the developed method and its suitability for reliable recognition of the tested analytes.

The two developed techniques have been examined for the determination of AC in tablets to test their practical applicability. For the DSF method, the results abridged in Table 5 show excellent mean recovery, %, equals 98.79 ± 2.86%. Meanwhile, the mean recovery, %, obtained using the PAD was 98.33 ± 3.21. Furthermore, the obtained results confirmed the absence of NP in both formulations where no quenching of GQDs fluorescence was observed by excitation at 350 nm. Hence, spiking experiments were performed by the addition of different concentrations of NP to AC tablets. The obtained mean recovery, %, of NP from AC tablets by the DSF was 99.77 ± 0.73%, while it equals 100.43 ± 1.25% using the PAD (Table 5). These results revealed that the new methods are reliable, showing good accuracy for the determination of AC in pharmaceutical formulations as well as detection of its toxic impurity NP.

Fig. S6a and S6b show representative images of a PAD used for the determination of AC samples captured under 254 and 365 nm UV-irradiation, respectively. In Fig. S6, the PAD integrates three sample detection zones (sample + GQDs), a blank zone (GQDs only), two standard control zones to include individual AC and NP standards of known concentrations (standard + GQDs), and two negative control zones (AC and NP only without GQDs). To demonstrate the PAD reliability and accuracy, two concentrations from AC tablet extract were dropped on two sample detection zones, and the third zone was spotted with AC tablet extract spiked with NP (Fig. S6).

For further demonstration of the validity of the developed DSF and PAD methods, a statistical comparison of the results of the proposed methods with those of a reported HPLC method [17] was performed. At first, the Brown-Forsythe test was used for testing variances equality, and no significant difference was observed between DSF, PAD, and reference HPLC method [17] in their variances, indicating the developed methods’ precision for the determination of AC and NP (Table 5). Then, the means of recovery, %, of DSF, PAD, and reference HPLC method [17] were compared with the ANOVA test. There were insignificant differences between the three tested methods regarding the mean recovery, %, for AC and NP. Furthermore, these results were confirmed using Dunnett's post hoc test, which compared each of the DSF and PAD methods with the reference HPLC method [17], and no significant difference was observed (Table 5). All the tests were two-sided at a probability of p > 0.05 [40,41]. The comparison results demonstrated the good performance of the developed DSF and PAD methods for the determination of AC and NP in pharmaceutical formulations without significant interference.

NP is well-known as a hazardous and a bio-accumulative compound with a profound endocrine-disrupting activity [42]. Furthermore, we investigated the ADMET (absorption, distribution, metabolism, excretion, toxicity) properties of NP via the admetSAR2 tool [43] and we revealed that it is a toxic compound belonging to the toxicity category II and a potentially carcinogenic substance. It is also predicted to have an androgen receptor binding potential, which is a reported effect of some other AC-related compounds [44]. These facts highlight the importance and significance of the developed techniques for rapid and cheap detection of this toxic impurity in AC as one of the most commonly administered analgesics.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Critical comparison of the proposed strategies and the reported methods for simultaneous determination of AC and NP
ce:section.ce:para: The comparison of the results achieved in the present analytical strategies and the earlier reported methods for simultaneous determination of AC and NP are abridged in Table 6. The bold advantage of the developed techniques is the outstanding sensitivity where the lower linear limits of the DSF method are 30–200 and 5–100 times more sensitive for AC and NP, respectively, than the reported methods [17–19], while the PAD method is 75–500 and 12.5–250 more sensitive for AC and NP, respectively than the reported HPLC literature [17–19]. This behavior is sensible since all of the reported methods for simultaneous analysis of AC and NP are based on HPLC coupled with UV-detection (Table 6) that is inherently less sensitive than the fluorescence measurement adapted in the DSF and PAD reported in this study.

Other distinctive advantages of the two developed strategies are their simplicity, dependence on un-sophisticated techniques, and using the minimum amounts of chemicals and reagents on contrary to the published techniques that consume plenty of organic solvents such as acetonitrile and methanol. Furthermore, the PAD technique is characterized by high sample throughput.

The PAD shows many distinguished advantages over all other techniques, including miniaturization, rapidness, lightweight, handiness, safe and economical disposability, very low cost, no need for highly skilled analysts, and a very simple fabrication procedure using cheap and easily obtained permanent markers. The developed PAD can be easily implemented in any laboratory, opening the door for point-of-detection fabrication of the desired devices with tailored designs.

Recently, it becomes very vital to consider the United Nations' sustainable goals while working in chemistry laboratories. Since the lack of system thinking may cause noble intents to produce unintentional counter-productive results, we employed the green analytical chemistry calculator, AGREE—Analytical GREEnness [45], to address the potential impacts of the developed techniques on human and environmental safety and to compare their greenness with that of the reported literature. This metric depends on evaluation of the analytical procedure for 12 principles: sample pretreatment activities, sample size and number, location of the analytical device, the extent of integration of the analytical steps, the extent of automation and miniaturization of the sample preparation steps, derivatization, volume and management of waste, the throughput of the analysis, energy intake, reagents source, toxicity of reagents, and safety of the worker. The result of this evaluation is a circular pictogram with a score from zero to one indicating the degree of the method greenness where a score equal to one indicates perfect greenness. Table 6 shows the results of the assessment of the published methods and the two developed strategies using the AGREE calculator. As illustrated in the obtained pictograms and scores, the developed DSF and PAD techniques are greener than all of the reported methods with scores of 0.67 and 0.83, respectively, while the scores of the reported literature ranged from 0.38 to 0.47. The main keys of the greenness of the developed techniques are using the minimum amounts of reagents that are inherently safe, low energy needs, high analysis throughput, omission of any derivatization steps, generation of minimum waste, minimal analysis steps, and safety to the analysts. Furthermore, the PAD was proved as the greenest and safest technique since it is characterized by miniaturization, very low sample and reagents consumption, great sample throughput since the number of detection zones/PAD can be easily increased as needed. Besides, the waste generated after analysis is the papers only that can be easily and safely disposed of by incineration.
The entire comparison study confirmed the superior position of the two established analytical strategies among the published literature for the determination of AC and NP.

This contribution has described, for the first time, a smart DSF and an in-lab-made device as two innovative techniques for simultaneous determination of AC and its endocrine disrupting impurity NP. The two techniques were based on the ability of AC and NP to quench the fluorescence of GQDs selectively via the IFE at λex of 240 and 350 nm, respectively, without any interference from one with the other. DSF at 240/440 and 350/440 nm permitted the selective and fast determination of AC and NP over the concentration ranges of 0.1–20.0 and 0.1–2.0 µg/mL, respectively. On the other hand, the in-lab-made device integrates a new PAD fabricated using a permanent ink marker, GQDs to create fluorescent sensing zones, and 254/365 nm handy UV-lamp fitted into an imaging chamber. The fabrication of the PAD via freehand drawing using the permanent ink marker offers various advantages where it is an instrument-free technique, it does not need any post-fabrication treatment, it is a very affordable and easily accessible method, and it is very simple with no need for a highly-skilled operator. Smartphone imaging of the PAD under UV-irradiation at 254 and 365 nm and coupling with smartphone-based image processing via the free RGB color detector software offers an independent platform with a great potentiality to be applied in any laboratory all over the world. The PAD has wide dynamic ranges for AC (0.04–6.0 µg/zone) and NP (0.04–2.0 µg/zone), respectively, with nanoscale detectability (LOD equals 13 ng/zone for AC and 9 ng/zone for NP). Potentially co-existing substances did not interfere with the determination of either AC or NP by the DSF and the PAD. The recovery of AC was 98.79 ± 2.86 and 98.33 ± 3.21 for real application in commercial tablets by the DSF and the PAD techniques, respectively, demonstrating the reliability and applicability of the developed methods.

Based on the findings detailed herein, we believe that the two proposed techniques are attractive tools for the selective and simultaneous determination of AC and NP simply and rapidly with no need for multiple steps or complicated procedures. These are the first techniques to employ the dual excitation approach to achieve this aim using a single type of nanomaterial. Furthermore, the designed PAD is the first lab-on-paper platform for simultaneous estimation of AC and NP.

Rania El-Shaheny: Conceptualization, Methodology, Validation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Resources, Data Curation, Visualization, Writing – original draft. Lateefa A. ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Rania El-Shaheny received her B.Sc., M.Sc., and Ph.D. degrees from the Faculty of Pharmacy, Mansoura University, Egypt. She is an associate professor at the Pharmaceutical Analytical Chemistry Department, Faculty of Pharmacy, Mansoura University since 2017. She is a visiting researcher at the Graduate School of Biomedical Sciences, Nagasaki University, from 2018 until now. Her research focuses on the development of new luminescence-based probes for clinically significant compounds in addition to developing probes for pharmaceuticals and environmental pollutants. Besides, her research interests included the usage of nanomaterials as nano extraction beds and nanoprobes for pharmaceuticals. Furthermore, her research included developing novel analytical techniques for investigating the safety, pharmacokinetics, and in vivo transformations of drugs.

Lateefa Al-khateeb received her B.Sc. and M.Sc. degrees from the Faculty of Sciences, King Abdulaziz University, Kingdom of Saudi Arabia, then received her Ph.D. degree from the Faculty of Sciences, Loughborough University, United Kingdom. Her research interests included the usage of nanomaterials as nano extraction beds and nanoprobes for pharmaceuticals, in addition to her work on and superheated water liquid chromatography and ion-selective electrodes.

Mahmoud Hamed El-Maghrabey received his B.Sc. and M.Sc. degrees from the Faculty of Pharmacy, Mansoura University, Egypt, then received his Ph.D. degree from the Graduate School of Biomedical Sciences, Nagasaki University, Japan. He is a Lecturer at the Pharmaceutical Analytical Chemistry Department, Faculty of Pharmacy, Mansoura University. Also, he is an Assistant Professor at the Graduate School of Biomedical Sciences, Nagasaki University. His research focuses on developing new detecting probes for bioactive substances, including pharmaceuticals and their potential impurities, metals, and pollutants. Besides, He developed many probes for mass spectrometric, fluorescence, and chemiluminescence analysis of clinically important compounds.
